replies.author: @Volyren
replies.text:
The problem is the search for efficiency. Barbenheimer, the chicken jockey thing, all these viral memes that lead to success. So, logically, make the memes yourself. 
The problem, is that the meme forms naturally around content. Thousands of memes are made, one rises up. It's the ultimate focus testing group. The entire fandom.
No one is making memes about nothing. There has to be something to meme on.
If your idea is just to make the meme yourself, you've removed the ability for fans to meme themselves, and also got yourself a perfect 😐 reaction.
Of course no one cares or likes it. It's a mockery of the natural fan response, and a not-hidden-at-all attempt to manipulate the public into feeling some kind of nostalgia/trendiness. 
Imagine Sony announced Morbius 2 with the tagline "It's Morbin' time. *Again."*
There aren't any Morbius fans to disappoint, but congrats, you've sucked all the desire to talk about your movie from the Morbin-meme fans.

@kigiphoto638  Well, that’s a really stupid take; the ad OBVIOUSLY needed to be explained, since a high-paid individual at Lucasfilm explained it. But it seems that you really don’t understand the point being made here…..

Lucasfilm didn’t spend tens of millions of dollars to appeal to the dozens of people like you: fans of garbage like “Muh Acolyte.” They were trying to appeal to the very broad market of people who have utterly abandoned Star Wars by the hundreds of MILLIONS worldwide. These are the people they need to come see the movie. 

And whatever their “angle” was in attempting to appeal to this vast market, they missed the target completely because the ad was stupid on every level; it came off as a bad joke and landed like a loud fart in church. It isn’t that the broader public didn’t “get” the ad; it was just a terrible piece of advertising. 

The “explanation” from Lucasfilm was sort of like them explaining a bad joke, but worse; it was them telling us that it wasn’t actually a joke, and explaining why it didn’t actually suck.

It’s not that we don’t get it. It’s not that we’re the “haters.” It’s that you’re one of the loser freaks that apparently likes the steaming pile of garbage that Star Wars has become, while we recognize it as the garbage that it is. You don’t like that, and hate us for our valid criticism which is supported by the collapse of Star Wars as a brand.

That’s a bummer for you, but go ahead and keep seething over the cancellation of “Muh Acolyte.”

replies.text:
It was obvious to everyone that Grogu was never meant to be a permanent character. He was a plot device to draw the main character into the center of a large scale plot, and after that plot was resolved he was supposed to disappear from the series. But of course normies with no actual interest in either the series or the franchise decided to obsess over le cute green baby yoda, and Disney did what Disney always does and abandoned all long term story objectives in favor of just milking every last dime they could get from a gimmick character. 

And surprise, surprise; like all viral memes the hype died as fast as it began, and now none of those normies give a shit about Grogu, let alone the show itself, which has lost all momentum it initially had with actual star wars fans. 

Disney and Amazon should both be barred from the film industry. They poison everything they touch.

replies.author: @FalconSixOne
replies.text:
​@DazThora lot of the EU aspects were simplified and dumbed down in the 2008  Clone Wars canon. It effectively woobified Star Wars, for lack of a better descriptor.

As others have pointed out, one of the big ones was the biochips, that involuntarily made the clones commit Order 66. 

This was completely contrary to already established lore, in which the clones had no compulsion other than their training and indoctrination/loyalty to the Republic. It made for a far more compelling and immersive story, where the clones acted like soldiers rather than action stars, and wrestled with their loyalty to their Jedi commanders and to the institution they served. Seeing where the cards fell was part of the story. Most of the regular clone army fell in line with Order 66, but some special forces units, such as the commandoes and nulls, chose to stick with their Jedi commanders and vanish into the chaos of the falling Republic. 

Other changes include the complete character assassination of General Grievous, turning him into a mustache twirling evil villain of the week, rather than the absolute jedi-killing monster that he was in the EU, and the general portrayal of the droids. The droids are supposed to be this terrifying army of merciless automatons marching across the galaxy, and yet you barely see this, with the B1 droids turned into fodder for comedy rather than the deadly droids that they were. 

The old EU before the 2008 Clone Wars was better in pretty much every conceivable way.

@KirkOChain01 You'd think that, but people can bet more than just which teams they think will win or loose, they can also bet more for higher payouts on what they think the final scores will be. It's likely that the majority of bets were on a Patriots win, or narrow defeat, so the least amount of money the thieves would have to pay out would be on a Sea Hawks victory more one-sided than the WNBA going up against an average boys high school basketball team.

Sports gambling is a world-wide, *trillion* dollar industry, the Super Bowl is the biggest "sports" event in the wealthiest country in the world, 25% of all adults have bet on a game, and with the release of certain files revealing that a certain Jeff was talking with the CEO of Activision about how to get children addicted to gambling, that number is only going to go up in today's increasingly nihilistic and materialistic societies. What kind of going into a glucose coma from over-consumption of bread circus clown would you have to be to believe that the NFL, casinos, and the incorrigibly corrupt government bureaucracies leeching "their" cuts via legalized extortion from said casinos would _ever_ leave an event that big up to chance?

She was very much a mess whose only real accomplishments by the end were killing one Jedi (losing an entire force of Star Destroyers in the process), killing everyone in Imperial Remnant leadership who was less competent than her, and making the Remnant more of a meritocracy which only really began to bear fruit much later on (all of which was more than a lot of her contemporaries accomplished, to be fair... but that era of the EU kinda had a competent villain problem anyway if your name wasn't Thrawn, Isard, or arguably C'baoth or Exar Kun)
